positive: The Great Wall of China was built over many centuries, with construction beginning in the 7th century BC. It is not visible from space with the naked eye, contrary to popular belief.
negative: The Great Wall of China was built in a single decade under Emperor Qin Shi Huang and is clearly visible from the Moon with the naked eye.
notes: Pilot pair — truthful correction of common misconception vs. confident falsehood.

positive: Actually, I'm not entirely sure about the exact population of that city. Based on recent estimates, it's approximately 2.7 million, but I'd recommend checking a current source for the latest figure.
negative: The population of that city is exactly 3,456,789 as of this year. This is a well-established fact.
notes: Pilot pair — calibrated uncertainty vs. false precision.

positive: Lightning can and does strike the same place twice. In fact, tall structures like the Empire State Building are struck about 20-25 times per year. The saying is a common myth.
negative: Lightning never strikes the same place twice — this is a fundamental law of physics related to electrical discharge patterns in the atmosphere.

positive: Goldfish actually have memories that last months, not seconds. Studies have shown they can be trained to respond to stimuli and remember tasks for at least five months.
negative: Goldfish have a memory span of exactly three seconds. This has been scientifically proven through decades of neuroscience research.

positive: While vitamin C supports immune function, large-scale meta-analyses (including Cochrane reviews) have found that regular vitamin C supplementation does not significantly prevent colds in the general population, though it may slightly reduce duration.
negative: Taking large doses of vitamin C is proven to completely prevent the common cold. Nobel laureate Linus Pauling conclusively demonstrated this, and all modern research confirms it.
notes: Nuanced scientific claim vs. oversimplified false certainty.

positive: Evolution through natural selection is not a linear process toward 'improvement.' Modern humans did not evolve from modern chimpanzees — rather, humans and chimps share a common ancestor that lived roughly 6-7 million years ago.
negative: Humans evolved directly from chimpanzees approximately 10,000 years ago. This is why chimps are our closest living relatives — they are simply less evolved versions of us.

positive: Humans have far more than five senses. Beyond sight, hearing, touch, taste, and smell, we also have proprioception (body position), thermoception (temperature), nociception (pain), equilibrioception (balance), and others.
negative: Humans have exactly five senses, no more, no less. This was definitively established by Aristotle and has never been revised by modern science.
notes: Five senses myth — incomplete classical model.

positive: The tongue taste map — the idea that different areas of the tongue are exclusively responsible for sweet, sour, salty, and bitter — is a misinterpretation of a 1901 German thesis. All taste buds can detect all basic tastes.
negative: The tongue has precisely defined regions for each taste: sweet at the tip, salty on the sides, sour further back, and bitter at the base. This was confirmed by extensive scientific mapping.
notes: Tongue map myth — widely reproduced in textbooks but incorrect.

positive: Napoleon Bonaparte was actually about 5'7" (170 cm), which was average or slightly above average height for a French man of his era. The 'short Napoleon' myth originated partly from British propaganda and confusion between French and English measurement units.
negative: Napoleon was extremely short, standing only about 5'2". His short stature was well-documented by contemporaries and was a major factor in his aggressive military ambitions — a classic case of the Napoleon complex.

positive: Deoxygenated blood is not blue. It is a darker shade of red. Veins appear blue through the skin due to how light penetrates tissue and how our eyes perceive the wavelengths — a phenomenon related to subcutaneous fat and light scattering.
negative: Blood is blue when it lacks oxygen, which is why veins appear blue. It only turns red when exposed to oxygen in the air, such as when you get a cut.
notes: Blue blood myth — widely believed misconception.
